Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit chaotic but I made it work for me. I'd block out time on weekends mostly, sometimes putting in five or six hours in one sitting because my weekdays were unpredictable. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, then sit down later to really process what I heard. I tried to keep a loose schedule of about 23 hours a week, but it definitely shifted around based on what was due.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on deadlines and how I was feeling that week. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, logging in every other day, but other weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline loomed. I've learned to be flexible with myself because rigid schedules just make my anxiety worse. When I had multiple assignments converging, I'd have to prioritize ruthlessly and sometimes that meant letting attendance slip.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about an upcoming deadline or wanting to watch a specific lecture video that prompted me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any announcements or changes I'd missed since my attendance wasn't great. I also used it to connect with peers when I needed clarification on something, which happened more often than I expected.
Q4: Yeah, there was a point mid-semester where logging in became almost automatic for me. I think it happened because I started using the video lectures as my main study tool, and I genuinely found them helpful. The mindfulness exercises I was doing helped me build better habits around checking in regularly. It felt less like a chore and more like my go-to resource once I got into that rhythm.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely my lifeline—I'd watch them multiple times, sometimes at different speeds. Being an auditory learner, hearing explanations really helped concepts stick better than reading ever did. I also found the peer interaction features useful because bouncing ideas off classmates helped me process information. The discussion boards were okay, though I didn't post much myself.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. I'd download them with good intentions but rarely actually read through them because reading just doesn't work as well for my learning style. Some of the optional quizzes I skipped too, especially when I was stressed and had to prioritize graded work. I know I probably missed some good content, but I had to be strategic about my energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to what I'd be assessed on or when it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. If I could listen to it rather than read it, that was a huge plus. I also valued activities that let me interact with other students because learning feels less isolating that way.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't quite afford, honestly. With my anxiety levels, I'd stress about whether I was spending time on the right things, so required work always took priority. When I did engage with optional stuff, it felt more relaxed and exploratory, but that was rare. Required activities came with this weight of consequence that optional ones just didn't have.
Q9: Oh absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also used external YouTube videos and podcasts to supplement topics I found confusing. My notes were all handwritten, and I'd spend hours reviewing them without ever touching the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't show it at all. Like when I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three times while taking notes, or when I'd have long study sessions with peers in person discussing concepts. I also did a lot of mental processing during my mindfulness practice, where I'd reflect on what I'd learned. None of that shows up anywhere.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or resource once, download it if possible, and then work with it offline for days. My internet is reliable, but I just preferred having materials available without needing to be logged in. It gave me flexibility to study whenever inspiration struck, even if I was somewhere without good wifi.
Q12: There were definitely low points, usually when my anxiety spiked around multiple deadlines converging. I'd feel paralyzed and avoid logging in because seeing all the tasks felt overwhelming. There was also a period when I was dealing with some personal stuff and my motivation just tanked. But I'm resilient—I always managed to pull myself back, even if my attendance suffered.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, I'd use the LMS in a very surface-level way—just checking what was due rather than actually engaging. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid starting things until I absolutely had to. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between attending live sessions and other commitments, which hurt my attendance rate.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. High anxiety does that to me—I'm technically present but not absorbing anything. I'd have to go back later when I was calmer and actually engage with the content properly. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though they showed as activity.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I'd like to admit, even important ones. My anxiety made starting things really hard because I'd worry about doing them wrong. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace, which sometimes meant waiting until the pressure of the deadline forced me to act. The mindfulness practice helped with this somewhat, but it's still a struggle.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd have bursts of intense activity in the days before something was due, then relative quiet afterward. It wasn't ideal, but that pressure was often what I needed to push through my anxiety and actually complete work. I've accepted this about myself even though I know it's not the healthiest approach.
Q17: Feedback had a big impact on me emotionally and practically. Positive feedback boosted my confidence and made me want to engage more with similar activities. Critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me hesitant to submit future work. I tried to use the mindfulness techniques to process feedback more objectively, which helped me stay resilient.
Q18: The course structure was a double-edged sword for me. Having lots of online courses gave me flexibility, which I needed, but it also meant I had to be very self-directed. The way modules were organized helped me see the big picture, but sometimes the sheer volume of content felt intimidating. I appreciated when there were clear pathways through the material.
Q19: The video-heavy format made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. Having materials available for download was crucial for my offline study habits. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or when there were too many small tasks that felt disconnected. Also, discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, which is why I only posted once.
Q20: I think that pattern makes total sense actually. Someone might be exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or dealing with anxiety about actually submitting work. They could be gathering information and processing it offline before feeling ready to produce something. High activity might also mean they're struggling to find what they need or feeling overwhelmed by the platform.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I performed reasonably well despite inconsistent LMS activity because so much of my real learning happened offline. Listening to recordings, studying with peers in person, and processing information during mindfulness practice—none of that shows up in logs. Someone's grade can reflect deep engagement that just isn't captured digitally.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access, timing around deadlines, and which resources get clicked on. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether someone was actually focused or just had a tab open. They miss offline study, peer discussions outside the platform, and the mental work of processing and connecting ideas. They show behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm genuinely curious about the material and actively trying to understand it, not just complete tasks. It's when I find myself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when I want to discuss ideas with others. For me, engagement also means managing my anxiety well enough to show up consistently, even when it's hard. It's about being mentally present, not just technically logged in.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much the mindfulness intervention helped me stay resilient through difficult weeks. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures while walking or the study sessions with peers that happened in coffee shops. My emotional journey—the anxiety I pushed through, the moments of genuine excitement about learning—none of that appears in any log. The data shows a fraction of who I am as a student.
